Their God (whom they have inherited intact from their remote Puritan ancestors) is a narrow, irrational, unjust, mean, revengeful, bigoted Person.

I'm sorry because I meant to write you a very entertaining letter this time.
Do you mean to tell me that you actually live in all that confusion?
I know you meant it kindly, and I think you're an old dear to take so much trouble for such a silly thing as a hat.
But if I sometimes seem thoughtless and ungrateful, I never mean it.
I don't think children ought to know the meaning of the word; it's odious, detestable.
They are not a bit universal, if you know what I mean.
And, Daddy, I found your letter waiting for me--pardon--I mean your secretary's.
That sounds a little impertinent, but I don't mean it so.
Yours always, Judy 7th December Dear Daddy-Long-Legs, Thank you for permission to visit Julia--I take it that silence means consent.
11th January I meant to write to you from the city, Daddy, but New York is an engrossing place.

campus.sentence: I like the girls and the teachers and the classes and the campus and the things to eat.

All the lights are out on the campus, but I drank black coffee and I can't go to sleep.
Judy 30th May Dear Daddy-Long-Legs, Did you ever see this campus?
I started to tell you about the campus.

We are supposed to be inside the campus at seven, but we are going to stretch a point tonight and make it eight.

corn.sentence: We have ice-cream twice a week and we never have corn-meal mush.

cream.sentence: We have ice-cream twice a week and we never have corn-meal mush.

We churn twice a week; and we keep the cream in the spring house which is made of stone with the brook running underneath.

I. We had written examinations this week in: A. Chemistry.
The country is Heaven after a week of rain.

They've had it two weeks; maybe they're thinking it over.

pepper.sentence: And I've been peppering you with letters every few days!
Dear me, Daddy, I seem to have a very ejaculatory style today; this page is peppered with exclamations.

adventure.sentence: But I've been so excited about all these new adventures that I MUST talk to somebody; and you're the only one I know.
There are such lots of adventures out in the fields!
In all the eighteen years I was there I only had one adventure--when the woodshed burned.
I regard it as a very unusual adventure.

excite.sentence: But I've been so excited about all these new adventures that I MUST talk to somebody; and you're the only one I know.
Whenever I think of it excited little thrills chase up and down my back.
It's really awfully queer not to know what one is--sort of exciting and romantic.
Did you ever hear of anything so exciting?
I am so excited over all these prospects that I can scarcely sleep.
Samuel was as excited about his clothes as any girl; he spent five times as much on dress as his wife--that appears to have been the Golden Age of husbands.
I was too amazed and excited and bewildered and happy.

exuberance.sentence: Please excuse my exuberance; I'll settle pretty soon.

settle.sentence: Please excuse my exuberance; I'll settle pretty soon.

soon.sentence: Please excuse my exuberance; I'll settle pretty soon.
(I'm going to commence being an author pretty soon.)

toss.sentence: If my letters bore you, you can always toss them into the wastebasket.

wastebasket.sentence: If my letters bore you, you can always toss them into the wastebasket.

november.sentence: I promise not to write another till the middle of November.

till.sentence: I promise not to write another till the middle of November.

listen.sentence: Yours most loquaciously, Judy Abbott 15th November Dear Daddy-Long-Legs, Listen to what I've learned to-day.

loquaciously.sentence: Yours most loquaciously, Judy Abbott 15th November Dear Daddy-Long-Legs, Listen to what I've learned to-day.

clothe.sentence: You've never heard about my clothes, have you, Daddy?
The bitterness of wearing your enemies' cast-off clothes eats into your soul.
It makes you want to devote your life to wearing clothes.
I felt as though they saw right through my sham new clothes to the checked ginghams underneath.
I plan it out to the littlest detail--the meals and clothes and study and amusements and punishments; for even my superior orphans are sometimes bad.

He wears knickerbockers and pleated jackets and white flannels and riding clothes with puffed trousers.

dress.sentence: Six dresses, all new and beautiful and bought for me--not handed down from somebody bigger.

But, Daddy, if you'd been dressed in checked ginghams all your life, you'd appreciate how I feel.

The grass is dotted with yellow dandelions and hundreds of girls in blue and white and pink dresses.

Julia and Sallie and I all had new dresses.
A very beautiful lady in a very beautiful velvet dress got into the car today, and without the slightest expression sat for fifteen minutes and looked at a sign advertising suspenders.

beautiful.sentence: Six dresses, all new and beautiful and bought for me--not handed down from somebody bigger.
I didn't know that people used to be monkeys and that the Garden of Eden was a beautiful myth.
I'm going to have a beautiful time in vacation; there's another Freshman who lives in Texas staying behind, and we are planning to take long walks and if there's any ice--learn to skate.

We had a beautiful time; I've longed for an uncle ever since.
I've been having the most beautiful vacation visiting Sallie.
I told him that I had spent last summer at Lock Willow, and we had a beautiful gossipy time about the Semples, and the horses and cows and chickens.
I have a beautiful play that I invented a long time ago when I first learned to read.
A perfectly beautiful lady with yellow hair and a long black silk trailing gown came to meet us with a welcoming smile.
She's growing up into an awfully energetic little hen--with a very determined cluck and lots of beautiful feathers (all due to you).
Our two Princeton men had a beautiful time--at least they politely said they had, and they've invited us to their dance next spring.
I've seen loads of theatres and hotels and beautiful houses.

A very beautiful lady in a very beautiful velvet dress got into the car today, and without the slightest expression sat for fifteen minutes and looked at a sign advertising suspenders.

The result is a beautiful commentary on human nature.
It's a beautiful day--frozen and icy and clear.
Give my kindest regards to Mrs. Lippett (that, I think, is truthful; love would be a little strong) and don't forget to tell her what a beautiful nature I've developed.

buy.sentence: Six dresses, all new and beautiful and bought for me--not handed down from somebody bigger.

I bought a new gown--one that I didn't need, but just wanted.

She sat down in front of a mirror and tried on a dozen, each lovelier than the last, and bought the two loveliest of all.
I can't imagine any joy in life greater than sitting down in front of a mirror and buying any hat you choose without having first to consider the price!
I have a new hat that I bought for twenty-five cents at the post office.

mark.sentence: Perhaps you don't realize what a climax that marks in the career of an orphan?

It looks well read--the marks of his grimy little hands are frequent!

career.sentence: Perhaps you don't realize what a climax that marks in the career of an orphan?

climax.sentence: Perhaps you don't realize what a climax that marks in the career of an orphan?

realize.sentence: Perhaps you don't realize what a climax that marks in the career of an orphan?

oblige.sentence: You gave them to me, and I am very, very, VERY much obliged.

His wife, who was an obliging creature, adopted 'dress reform.'
Amasai, who used to be so obliging about beating rugs and carrying wood, grumbles if you suggest such a thing.

own.sentence: It's a fine thing to be educated--but nothing compared to the dizzying experience of owning six new dresses.

Mr. McBride owns a factory and Christmas eve he had a tree for the employees' children.
I can walk over everybody's land, and look at everybody's view, and dabble in everybody's brook; and enjoy it just as much as though I owned the land--and with no taxes to pay!

dizzy.sentence: It's a fine thing to be educated--but nothing compared to the dizzying experience of owning six new dresses.
It's a dizzying experience, Daddy, to pass eighteen years in the John Grier Home, and then suddenly to be plunged into the WORLD.

fine.sentence: It's a fine thing to be educated--but nothing compared to the dizzying experience of owning six new dresses.
On the topmost branches of her family tree there's a superior breed of monkeys with very fine silky hair and extra long tails.
There's no doubt about it, Daddy; New York would rapidly undermine this fine stoical character which the John Grier Home so patiently built up.
It's a fine thing to win an event for your class, because the class that wins the most gets the athletic cup for the year.
It's a fine chance for me to learn housekeeping.
The woods are fine, and so is the camp, and so is the weather, and so are the McBrides, and so is the whole world.
'Today came home my fine Camlett cloak with gold buttons, which cost me much money, and I pray God to make me able to pay for it.'

compare.sentence: It's a fine thing to be educated--but nothing compared to the dizzying experience of owning six new dresses.
Sallie has one and Julia and Leonora each two, and they were all comparing them tonight.
You can imagine how bright she is compared with the rest of us--irregular verbs are mere playthings.

thank.sentence: Miss Pritchard, who is on the visiting committee, picked them out--not Mrs. Lippett, thank goodness.
I meant this to be just a short little thank-you note--but when I get started I seem to have a ready pen.
Goodbye, and thank you for thinking of me--I should be perfectly happy except for one little threatening cloud on the horizon.
Thank you for making a very sick, cross, miserable Freshman cheerful.
Thank heaven I don't inherit God from anybody!
It's a relief not having to thank Somebody for every mouthful you eat.
(I dare say I'm blasphemous; but you'd be, too, if you'd offered as much obligatory thanks as I have.)
I had a new white evening gown (your Christmas present--many thanks) and long white gloves and white satin slippers.
Thank you very much, but I do not feel that I can keep it.
In my heart I thank you always for the life and freedom and independence that you have given me.
Yours always, Judy 7th December Dear Daddy-Long-Legs, Thank you for permission to visit Julia--I take it that silence means consent.
He's a Socialist--except, thank Heaven, he doesn't let his hair grow and wear red ties.

goodness.sentence: Miss Pritchard, who is on the visiting committee, picked them out--not Mrs. Lippett, thank goodness.

oh.sentence: That wouldn't be an awfully big wardrobe for Julia Rutledge Pendleton, perhaps, but for Jerusha Abbott--Oh, my!
Let me see if I can't think of something else pleasant-- Oh, yes!
Oh, I tell you, Daddy, when we women get our rights, you men will have to look alive in order to keep yours.
Of course we're pleased--but oh, if we could only beat the juniors!
Jimmie McBride is going to teach me how to ride horseback and paddle a canoe, and how to shoot and--oh, lots of things I ought to know.
Our study is better than ever this year--faces the South with two huge windows and oh!
Oh, I tell you, Daddy, your little foundling is getting to be quite a prominent person!
And as for Florence, she is hopeless--but oh!
It's the most beautiful book you ever saw--Oh, pardon--I said that before.
Oh, dearest, if that had happened, the light would have gone out of the world for me.
I drove back to Lock Willow in the dark but oh, how the stars were shining!

awfully.sentence: That wouldn't be an awfully big wardrobe for Julia Rutledge Pendleton, perhaps, but for Jerusha Abbott--Oh, my!
Especially for me, because it was so awfully different from the asylum--I feel like an escaped convict every time I leave the campus.
It's awfully hard for me not to tell everything I know.
Will you be awfully disappointed, Daddy, if I don't turn out to be a great author?
Getting an education is an awfully wearing process!
It makes me almost hope I'm not a genius; they must be very wearing to have about--and awfully destructive to the furniture.
It's really awfully queer not to know what one is--sort of exciting and romantic.
Then straight away over two miles of rolling meadow, and awfully hard to follow, for the confetti was getting sparse.
It would be an awfully embarrassing mistake if I picked out the wrong one.
But you did once, when I was ill in the infirmary, send me a message, and now, when I am feeling awfully forgotten, I get out your card and read it over.
Our ideas there were bounded by the four sides of the iron fence, only I didn't mind it so much because I was younger, and was so awfully busy.
He is an awfully companionable sort of man, though you would never believe it to see him casually; he looks at first glance like a true Pendleton, but he isn't in the least.
It's awfully funny to think of that great big, long-legged man (he's nearly as long-legged as you, Daddy) ever sitting in Mrs. Semple's lap and having his face washed.
That's a mountain near here; not an awfully high mountain, perhaps--no snow on the summit--but at least you are pretty breathless when you reach the top.
She was awfully funny; you would have thought that we were ten years old, and she a distracted mother.
She is awfully troubled to think that she didn't train him better when he was small and helpless and she had the chance.
When you get accustomed to people or places or ways of living, and then have them snatched away, it does leave an awfully empty, gnawing sort of sensation.
A storm is awfully disturbing in the country.
I am awfully glad, Daddy, because now I won't be such a burden to you.
She's growing up into an awfully energetic little hen--with a very determined cluck and lots of beautiful feathers (all due to you).

wardrobe.sentence: That wouldn't be an awfully big wardrobe for Julia Rutledge Pendleton, perhaps, but for Jerusha Abbott--Oh, my!

waste.sentence: I suppose you're thinking now what a frivolous, shallow little beast she is, and what a waste of money to educate a girl?

beast.sentence: I suppose you're thinking now what a frivolous, shallow little beast she is, and what a waste of money to educate a girl?

shallow.sentence: I suppose you're thinking now what a frivolous, shallow little beast she is, and what a waste of money to educate a girl?

conceited.sentence: You don't think me conceited, do you, Daddy dear?

enthusiastic.sentence: I'm not, really, only just now I'm in the enthusiastic stage.

sniffy.sentence: Maybe later on I'll get cold and critical and sniffy.

ash.sentence: She used to laugh when he tramped in mud or dropped ashes on the floor, but now--you should hear her scold!

mud.sentence: She used to laugh when he tramped in mud or dropped ashes on the floor, but now--you should hear her scold!

grumble.sentence: Amasai, who used to be so obliging about beating rugs and carrying wood, grumbles if you suggest such a thing.

suggest.sentence: Amasai, who used to be so obliging about beating rugs and carrying wood, grumbles if you suggest such a thing.

dingy.sentence: Also his neckties are quite dingy--black and brown, where they used to be scarlet and purple.

scarlet.sentence: Also his neckties are quite dingy--black and brown, where they used to be scarlet and purple.

content.sentence: The pigs are unusually fat, the cows seem contented and the hens are laying well.

eggs.sentence: If so, let me recommend that invaluable little work, 200 Eggs per Hen per Year.

invaluable.sentence: If so, let me recommend that invaluable little work, 200 Eggs per Hen per Year.

trollope.sentence: I have decided to stay until I've written 114 novels like Anthony Trollope's mother.

retire.sentence: Then I shall have completed my life work and can retire and travel.

james.sentence: Mr. James McBride spent last Sunday with us.

fried.sentence: Fried chicken and ice-cream for dinner, both of which he appeared to appreciate.

momentary.sentence: I was awfully glad to see him; he brought a momentary reminder that the world at large exists.

reminder.sentence: I was awfully glad to see him; he brought a momentary reminder that the world at large exists.

peddle.sentence: Poor Jimmie is having a hard time peddling his bonds.

job.sentence: I think he'll end up by going home to Worcester and taking a job in his father's factory.

hearted.sentence: He's too open and confiding and kind-hearted ever to make a successful financier.

financier.sentence: He's too open and confiding and kind-hearted ever to make a successful financier.

successful.sentence: He's too open and confiding and kind-hearted ever to make a successful financier.

overall.sentence: But to be the manager of a flourishing overall factory is a very desirable position, don't you think?
Just now he turns up his nose at overalls, but he'll come to them.

manager.sentence: But to be the manager of a flourishing overall factory is a very desirable position, don't you think?

cramp.sentence: I hope you appreciate the fact that this is a long letter from a person with writer's cramp.

pint.sentence: With beautiful scenery all about, and lots to eat and a comfortable four-post bed and a ream of blank paper and a pint of ink--what more does one want in the world?

scenery.sentence: With beautiful scenery all about, and lots to eat and a comfortable four-post bed and a ream of blank paper and a pint of ink--what more does one want in the world?

ream.sentence: With beautiful scenery all about, and lots to eat and a comfortable four-post bed and a ream of blank paper and a pint of ink--what more does one want in the world?

suffer.sentence: That's a very pleasant prospect--only I am afraid my poor book will suffer.

wonder.sentence: 27th August Dear Daddy-Long-Legs, Where are you, I wonder?

peak.sentence: I hope you're on a mountain peak (but not in Switzerland; somewhere nearer) looking at the snow and thinking about me.

switzerland.sentence: I hope you're on a mountain peak (but not in Switzerland; somewhere nearer) looking at the snow and thinking about me.

settlement.sentence: Sallie is going to do settlement work in Boston next winter.

boston.sentence: Sallie is going to do settlement work in Boston next winter.
But truly, Daddy, I think I shall have to go to Boston.

studio.sentence: Don't you think it would be nice for me to go with her, then we could have a studio together?
I know in advance that you won't like my studio idea.

settled.sentence: I would write while she SETTLED and we could be together in the evenings.

evenings.sentence: Evenings are very long when there's no one but the Semples and Carrie and Amasai to talk to.

griggs.sentence: I am certain that a man named Elmer H. Griggs must be horrid.

elmer.sentence: I am certain that a man named Elmer H. Griggs must be horrid.

sheer.sentence: If something doesn't happen soon, I shall throw myself into the silo pit out of sheer desperation.

pit.sentence: If something doesn't happen soon, I shall throw myself into the silo pit out of sheer desperation.

desperation.sentence: If something doesn't happen soon, I shall throw myself into the silo pit out of sheer desperation.

dusty.sentence: All the grass is burnt up and the brooks are dry and the roads are dusty.

hydrophobia.sentence: This letter sounds as though I had hydrophobia, but I haven't.

affair.sentence: I am so sorry that you have been ill; I wouldn't have bothered you with my affairs if I had known.

serially.sentence: It's going to be published serially in seven parts, and then in a book!

part.sentence: It's going to be published serially in seven parts, and then in a book!

wild.sentence: You might think I'd be wild with joy, but I'm not.

overgrow.sentence: In other ways, though, he's just an overgrown boy, and he does need looking after--he hasn't any sense about wearing rubbers when it rains.

antagonistic.sentence: He and I always think the same things are funny, and that is such a lot; it's dreadful when two people's senses of humour are antagonistic.

gulf.sentence: I don't believe there's any bridging that gulf!

explain.sentence: If you have, I don't need to explain; if you haven't, I can't explain.
I never told him about the orphan asylum, and I hated to explain that I didn't know who I was.
Suppose I go to him and explain that the trouble isn't Jimmie, but is the John Grier Home--would that be a dreadful thing for me to do?

dumb.sentence: I didn't tell him why; I was just dumb and miserable.

misunderstand.sentence: But Master Jervie and I got into a dreadful muddle of misunderstanding and we both hurt each other's feelings.

antecedent.sentence: It didn't seem right for a person of my lack of antecedents to marry into any such family as his.

proud.sentence: And his family are proud--and I'm proud, too!

unconventional.sentence: Of course he is a Socialist, and he has unconventional ideas; maybe he wouldn't mind marrying into the proletariat so much as some men might.

unemotional.sentence: But I'd like to get your unemotional opinion.

stir.sentence: I was just getting sort of acclimated to the feeling of a broken heart, when a letter came from Julia that stirred me all up again.

blankness.sentence: I was feeling hurt because he had just disappeared into blankness without a word.

certainly.sentence: Judy 6th October Dearest Daddy-Long-Legs, Yes, certainly I'll come--at half-past four next Wednesday afternoon.

tangible.sentence: I can't believe that I am really going to see you--I've been just THINKING you so long that it hardly seems as though you are a tangible flesh-and-blood person.
I want to have you close by where I can touch you and make sure you are tangible.

blood.sentence: I can't believe that I am really going to see you--I've been just THINKING you so long that it hardly seems as though you are a tangible flesh-and-blood person.

flesh.sentence: I can't believe that I am really going to see you--I've been just THINKING you so long that it hardly seems as though you are a tangible flesh-and-blood person.

faint.sentence: I'm afraid of butlers, and if one opens the door I shall faint upon the step.

butler.sentence: I'm afraid of butlers, and if one opens the door I shall faint upon the step.
But I needn't have been a bit afraid; your butler is such a nice, fatherly old man that he made me feel at home at once.

cab.sentence: When the doctor came down yesterday to put me in the cab, he told me that for three days they gave you up.

doctor.sentence: When the doctor came down yesterday to put me in the cab, he told me that for three days they gave you up.
The doctor said I must be a good nurse, that you looked ten years younger.
It was a very sweet half hour before your doctor came and sent me away.

precious.sentence: Always before I could be frivolous and care-free and unconcerned, because I had nothing precious to lose.

unconcerned.sentence: Always before I could be frivolous and care-free and unconcerned, because I had nothing precious to lose.

darling.sentence: Will you still care for me, darling, if I turn out to be only eleven?

wonderful.sentence: Yesterday was the most wonderful day that could ever happen.

dawn.sentence: The girl that left Lock Willow at dawn was a very different person from the one who came back at night.

darkness.sentence: I started wide awake in the darkness and the first thought that popped into my head was, 'I am going to see Daddy-Long-Legs!'

wide.sentence: I started wide awake in the darkness and the first thought that popped into my head was, 'I am going to see Daddy-Long-Legs!'

glorious.sentence: I ate breakfast in the kitchen by candle-light, and then drove the five miles to the station through the most glorious October colouring.

station.sentence: I ate breakfast in the kitchen by candle-light, and then drove the five miles to the station through the most glorious October colouring.
I was so dazed when I got to the station that I almost took a train for St Louis.

rail.sentence: All the way in the train the rails kept singing, 'You're going to see Daddy-Long-Legs.'

faith.sentence: I had such faith in Daddy's ability to set things right.

journey.sentence: And I knew that somewhere another man--dearer than Daddy--was wanting to see me, and somehow I had a feeling that before the journey ended I should meet him, too.

block.sentence: When I came to the house on Madison Avenue it looked so big and brown and forbidding that I didn't dare go in, so I walked around the block to get up my courage.

sombre.sentence: It was a very sombre, magnificent, man's sort of room.

magnificent.sentence: It was a very sombre, magnificent, man's sort of room.

edge.sentence: I sat down on the edge of a big upholstered chair and kept saying to myself: 'I'm going to see Daddy-Long-Legs!

presently.sentence: Then presently the man came back and asked me please to step up to the library.

knock.sentence: Then he knocked and said, 'Miss Abbott,' and I went in and the door closed behind me.

shakily.sentence: Before I could stop him he rose--rather shakily--and steadied himself by the back of the chair and just looked at me without a word.

wit.sentence: A hundred little things might have told me, if I had had any wits.
